title: SEGMENT 6 — Clopidogrel and CYP2C19: the prodrug that doesn't work for everyone

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Clopidogrel and CYP2C19: the prodrug that doesn't work for everyone
Now let me tell you a pharmacogenomics story where the clinical benefit is, by contrast, unambiguous.
Clopidogrel — brand name Plavix — and the enzyme CYP2C19.
Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet drug.
Platelets are the little sticky cell fragments in your blood that initiate clotting.

After certain cardiovascular procedures — most famously, after a doctor places a stent in a coronary artery to keep it open — you absolutely must keep your platelets from clumping onto the stent, because if they do, the stent itself becomes the trigger for a clot, and that clot in a coronary artery is a heart attack.
Stent thrombosis.
It can kill you within hours.
Clopidogrel is the standard drug for preventing this.
It binds to a receptor on the platelet called P2Y12 and blocks platelet activation.
Hundreds of millions of prescriptions a year.
One of the top-selling drugs in pharmaceutical history.
Here's the catch.
Clopidogrel, as you swallow it, is inactive.
It's a prodrug.
It does nothing to your platelets in the form you take it.
To work, it has to be metabolically activated in the liver — specifically, it has to be processed through CYP2C19 to generate the active metabolite that binds the P2Y12 receptor.
No CYP2C19, no active drug.
No active drug, no antiplatelet effect.
Now think about what this means.
Remember the metabolizer phenotypes from Segment 4.
A patient who is a CYP2C19 poor metabolizer — two no-function alleles, the most common of which is CYP2C19\*2 — barely activates clopidogrel.
The prodrug they swallow stays a prodrug.
It circulates in their blood, gets cleared by other pathways, and never converts efficiently to the active form.
So the drug, in their body, doesn't work.
They are getting essentially a placebo dose of antiplatelet medication.
And they don't know it.
Their cardiologist doesn't know it, unless they tested.
And a couple of weeks after the stent goes in, those patients have a substantially higher rate of stent thrombosis, heart attack, and death.
How common is this?
CYP2C19\2 is one of the most common loss-of-function variants in human pharmacogenomics.
In European populations, about 2% of people are \2/\2 — full poor metabolizers — and another 25% or so carry one \2 allele, making them intermediate metabolizers with reduced but not zero activation.
In East Asian populations, the numbers are dramatically higher.
About 14% of East Asians are CYP2C19 poor metabolizers — \2/\2 or \2/\3.
One in seven.

In a busy cardiology practice in Beijing or Seoul, you can do the math: a substantial fraction of patients leaving the hospital with a fresh stent and a prescription for clopidogrel are receiving a drug that won't work for them.
In 2010, after a series of studies and meta-analyses confirmed this effect, the FDA put a boxed warning on clopidogrel.
A boxed warning, also called a black box warning, is the strongest cautionary label the FDA can attach to a drug short of pulling it from the market.
The clopidogrel boxed warning explicitly states that CYP2C19 poor metabolizers may not benefit from the standard dose and that alternative therapy or alternative strategies should be considered.
What are the alternative strategies?
There are two main ones.
You can give a higher dose of clopidogrel — but this doesn't fully fix the problem and increases bleeding risk.
Or you can switch to a different antiplatelet — prasugrel or ticagrelor, both of which are activated through different pathways and aren't dependent on CYP2C19.
These drugs cost more and have their own side effect profiles, but for a CYP2C19 poor metabolizer they're the right choice.
So now the workflow.
A patient comes into the cath lab, gets a stent placed, and is about to be discharged with a prescription for antiplatelet therapy.
Before that prescription gets written, a CYP2C19 genotype is run — buccal swab, saline rinse, blood draw, doesn't matter, just need a few cells.
The result comes back, usually within a day.
If the patient is a normal or rapid metabolizer, clopidogrel is fine.
If the patient is an intermediate or poor metabolizer, they get prasugrel or ticagrelor instead.
This protocol is now standard at many major cardiology centers.
It's one of the cleanest pharmacogenomics implementations in practice.
The clinical benefit is measurable: fewer stent thromboses, fewer heart attacks, fewer deaths.
The test is cheap — a few tens of dollars for genotyping the relevant CYP2C19 SNPs.
The turnaround is fast.
The decision rule is simple: PM or IM, use a different drug.

If you want a single example of what pharmacogenomics, done well, looks like in real clinical practice — what it looks like when the science actually translates into changed care — this is the example.
Clopidogrel-CYP2C19.
The prodrug that doesn't work for everyone, but for which we can identify in advance who it won't work for.
There's one more thing about this story worth emphasizing.
Notice the population genetics.
The fact that CYP2C19\*2 is twice as common in East Asian populations as in European populations means that the clinical benefit of testing is distributed unequally across ancestries.
In a European population, a small fraction of patients will be identified as poor metabolizers and re-routed.
In an East Asian population, a much larger fraction will be.
We'll come back to this — the ancestry dimension of pharmacogenomics — because it's one of the deepest equity issues in the field.
